Mordecai refused to honor Haman. The religion of a Jew forbade him from giving honors to any mortal man that suggested idolatry, especially to so wicked a man as Haman. By nature all are idolaters; self is our favorite idol, we are pleased to be treated as if everything were at our disposal. Though religion does not destroy good manners, but teaches us to render honor to whom honor is due, yet by a citizen of Zion, both in his heart and in his eyes, such a vile person as Haman was, is despised. The true believer cannot obey edicts or conform to fashions that break the law of God. He must obey God rather than man, and leave the consequences to him. Haman was full of wrath. His plan was inspired by that wicked spirit, who has been a murderer from the beginning; whose enmity to Christ and his church governs all his children.

Without some understanding of the human heart and the history of mankind, we would not think that any prince could consent to a dreadful proposal so hurtful to himself. Let us be thankful for mild and just government. Haman inquires, according to his own superstitions, how to find a lucky day for the intended massacre! God’s wisdom serves its own purposes by men's folly. Haman has appealed to the lot, and the lot, by delaying the execution, gives judgment against him. The event explains the doctrine of a particular providence over all the affairs of men, and the care of God over his church. Haman was afraid that the king’s conscience would smite him for what he had done; to prevent this, he kept him drinking. This cursed method many often take to drown convictions and to harden their own hearts and the hearts of others in sin. All appeared to be going favorably to accomplish the project. But though sinners are permitted to proceed to the point they aim at, an unseen but almighty Power turns them back. How vain and contemptible are the strongest assaults against Jehovah! Had Haman obtained his wish, and the Jewish nation perished, what must have become of all the promises? How could the prophecies concerning the great Redeemer of the world have been fulfilled? Thus the everlasting covenant itself must have failed before this diabolical project could take place.
